what_it_does: This file is a living contract ledger that maps transport identifiers to their protocol, network exposure, caller identity, and inventory source for company code/design substrates. It records how each binding reaches a capability, who can call it, and where the canonical list of operations is maintained, including honest gaps, external dependencies, and removed routes.
observations: Exposure values are hand-verified because tools/extract_reality.py is NOT YET BUILT; the V24 machine-diff is an open obligation recorded under a fail-loud rule.
mcp-company uses MCP over stdio via FastMCP in mcp_face/server.py, is process-local, and requires explicit from_session rather than ambient caller identity.
supervisor-http is an HTTP/JSON service on 127.0.0.1:8771, localhost-only, anonymous-local, with routes drawn from SUPERVISOR_ROUTES in runtime/session_supervisor.py.
bridge-http serves HTTP/JSON + SSE on 127.0.0.1:8770, is localhost-only with selected Tailscale paths, and had 18 /api/config|dev|auto wrapper routes removed in Session Fabric R1.4 on 2026-06-13.

wire-job is a headless claude -p run-and-capture job reached indirectly through bridge-http POST /api/resolve, defaults to plan permission mode, and has no exported machine-readable command registry yet.
cli-local is the company console binary; like wire-job, it lacks an exported machine-readable command registry constant.
tui-interactive and agent-sdk are declared as non-fabric F2 surfaces with status planned and exposure n/a because no company-owned machine-readable inventory exists.
substrate-mcp is an external knowledge-vault MCP server the company depends on but does not own, marked F6 building and verified-by-use.
anthropic-admin-api is an external authed HTTPS API that the company does not proxy.
claude-cli and claude-tui are declared as non-fabric F4 management surfaces with status planned and no company inventory.
standouts: It explicitly catalogs surfaces the company does NOT expose (interactive TUI, Agent SDK, claude management subcommands) rather than omitting them.
It records unbuilt tooling and missing registries as first-class obligations instead of silently pretending they exist.
It documents a deliberate mass removal of bridge-http wrapper routes with the rationale that real Claude Code sessions already provide those capabilities.
It clearly separates company-owned transports from external ones (substrate-mcp, anthropic-admin-api).
conventions: Each transport entry follows a fixed schema: Protocol, Exposure, Caller identity, Inventory source, plus status notes.
References contract-format section numbers (§9.1, §9.2, §9.3, §5.3, §8) and acceptance tests as drift teeth.
Cites ground-truth files and approximate line numbers for implementation details.
Uses honest-gap annotations (HONEST GAP, HONEST REALITY) and a fail-loud tone.
Date-stamped living-document header with captured date and status.
Exposure values are registry keys meant to come from a generated exposure.json artifact.
concerns: Many entries depend on hand-verified values and not-yet-built tooling, creating a real risk of stale drift before the extractor lands.
The file mixes current state, deletion history, future migration plans, and external dependency notes, which may make scanning for current bindings harder.
Several transports are declared with no machine-readable inventory source, so the contract cannot yet be mechanically validated against them.
It declares itself as F1 transports but contains substantial F2/F4/F6 content, requiring careful scope discipline.
notes: A deliberately honest transport catalog that records not only reachable endpoints but also missing, external, and removed surfaces so fabric bindings cannot silently assume capabilities.
questions: Where is the contract-format document that defines the § references?
Who owns and when will tools/extract_reality.py be built to close the V24 machine-diff obligation?
How will exposure.json registry keys be generated and kept in sync once the extractor exists?
What criteria determine when a planned F2/F4 binding migrates to bridge-http or agent-sdk?
Which specific bridge-http paths are exposed over Tailscale and what exposure registry keys do they map to?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to map transport ids to protocol, exposure, caller identity, and inventory source; the file does that and also records gaps, removals, external dependencies, and non-fabric surfaces, which serves the same purpose more defensively.
apparent_themes: Radical honesty about unsupported or unbuilt surfaces.
Ground-truth anchoring with file and line citations.
Clear separation of company-owned and external transports.
Living contract with versioned removals and planned migrations.
Drift prevention through acceptance-test teeth and future machine extraction.
intention_signals: The file is trying to prevent the fabric contract from lying about reachability: every binding should cite a real transport with a real inventory source, and gaps should be visible obligations rather than hidden assumptions.
novelty: It inverts the typical API catalog by prominently listing things the company does not expose (interactive surfaces, external APIs, removed wrapper routes) and treating missing machine-readable inventory as a first-class recorded gap.
contribution: It contributes a transport-identity layer that distinguishes company-owned, external, interactive, and removed surfaces, and it contributes a pattern of loud gap-recording that a fused system can use to avoid phantom bindings.
summary_for_embedding: TRANSPORTS.md is a living contract ledger mapping transport identifiers to protocol, exposure, caller identity, and inventory source for company fabric and non-fabric surfaces, including honest gaps, external dependencies, and removed routes.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to make every binding's transport reachability and inventory source explicit and auditable, while loudly recording unbuilt extractors and unsupported interactive surfaces so they are not silently assumed.
